question: A 55-year-old male is sitting in tripod position unable to lie down. He is breathing 28 times per minute using accessory muscles. He can only speak 3-4 words at a time. Lung sounds reveal wheezing throughout. SpO2 is 88%. History of asthma. What is happening and what should you do?
option_a: He is having a panic attack
option_b: Severe asthma exacerbation with respiratory distress. Apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather. Assist with prescribed inhaler if available. Prepare for possible assisted ventilation if he tires. Transport urgently
option_c: His breathing is adequate since he is alert
option_d: Encourage him to lie down and relax
rationale: Tripod position accessory muscle use inability to speak in sentences tachypnea wheezing and hypoxia indicate severe respiratory distress from asthma. Provide high-flow oxygen assist with prescribed inhaler and transport. Monitor for deterioration requiring ventilatory assistance.

question: You are treating the asthma patient. He has his albuterol inhaler. He asks you to help him use it. What is the correct technique for assisting with a metered-dose inhaler?
option_a: Spray it into his mouth while he breathes in
option_b: Have patient exhale fully shake inhaler place in mouth seal lips around it then inhale slowly and deeply while pressing canister. Hold breath for 10 seconds then exhale slowly. Use spacer if available
option_c: Have him sniff the medication through his nose
rationale: Correct MDI technique maximizes medication delivery to lungs. Exhale fully first. Place inhaler in mouth or use spacer. Press canister while inhaling slowly and deeply. Hold breath 10 seconds to allow deposition. Spacers improve delivery especially in distressed patients.

question: A patient with severe asthma has been wheezing loudly. After several minutes of distress his wheezing suddenly becomes much quieter but he appears more tired and his SpO2 is dropping. Is the quiet wheezing a good sign?
option_a: Yes it means the attack is ending
option_b: No. Decreasing wheezing with worsening condition indicates severe bronchospasm with minimal air movement. This is a silent chest and indicates impending respiratory failure. Prepare for assisted ventilation and transport immediately
option_c: He is getting better so continue monitoring
rationale: Wheezing requires airflow. Decreasing wheeze with worsening symptoms indicates severe bronchospasm with almost no air movement which is a pre-arrest condition. A silent chest in asthma is an ominous sign requiring immediate intervention and transport.

question: A 68-year-old female with CHF woke suddenly unable to breathe. She must sit upright. She is coughing up pink frothy sputum. Lung sounds reveal crackles throughout. BP is 178/104. What is causing her symptoms?
option_a: Pneumonia
option_b: Acute pulmonary edema from heart failure. Fluid is backing up into her lungs. Position her sitting upright apply high-flow oxygen. If CPAP is available and she can cooperate it can help. Transport rapidly
option_c: Asthma attack
rationale: Pink frothy sputum waking suddenly unable to breathe orthopnea and bilateral crackles with history of CHF indicate acute pulmonary edema. The heart cannot pump effectively causing fluid to back up into lungs. Position upright apply high-flow oxygen and transport.

question: A 70-year-old male with COPD is in respiratory distress. He is on home oxygen. His daughter asks why you do not give him more oxygen since he is struggling. She says the doctor told him too much oxygen could be dangerous. How do you explain this?
option_a: Her father can never have more than 2 LPM oxygen
option_b: Some COPD patients rely on low oxygen levels to stimulate breathing. However we never withhold oxygen from someone who is hypoxic. We will give enough oxygen to help him breathe better while monitoring him carefully. Hypoxia is more immediately dangerous than the theoretical risk
option_c: Just agree that COPD patients cannot have oxygen
rationale: Explain that while some COPD patients can have issues with high oxygen the immediate risk of hypoxia is greater. We titrate oxygen to maintain adequate saturation around 88-92% while monitoring. Never withhold oxygen from a hypoxic patient regardless of COPD status.

question: A child with a history of croup now has a barking cough and inspiratory stridor. He is working hard to breathe with retractions. He is sitting up and crying. His SpO2 is 93%. What should you do?
option_a: Lay him down for better assessment
option_b: Keep him calm and in position of comfort usually sitting. Provide humidified oxygen if available. Do not agitate him as crying worsens stridor and oxygen demand. Transport gently
option_c: Force him to lie down
rationale: Croup causes upper airway swelling and stridor. Keep the child calm in position of comfort. Agitation and crying increase oxygen demand and worsen stridor. Provide humidified oxygen gently. Transport without causing distress. Calm child does better than upset child.

question: A 5-year-old child has sudden onset of high fever drooling difficulty swallowing and is sitting forward with neck extended. He looks very ill and has stridor. He has not been able to talk. What condition should you suspect and what should you avoid?
option_a: Croup. Look in his throat to confirm
option_b: Suspect epiglottitis. Do NOT examine the throat or agitate the child as this can trigger complete airway obstruction. Allow position of comfort provide blow-by oxygen keep him calm and transport immediately
option_c: He needs abdominal thrusts for choking
option_d: Give him water for his dry throat
rationale: Rapid onset high fever drooling tripod posture and stridor suggest epiglottitis. Do NOT look in the throat or agitate the child which can trigger complete obstruction. Keep calm allow position of comfort and transport immediately for emergency airway management.

question: An 18-year-old was stung by a bee and now has facial swelling difficulty breathing and hives. Her voice sounds hoarse and she says her throat is swelling. SpO2 is 91%. What condition is this and what does she need?
option_a: Minor allergic reaction. Give antihistamines
option_b: Anaphylaxis with airway involvement. She needs epinephrine immediately. Assist with her EpiPen if she has one or administer epinephrine per protocol. Apply high-flow oxygen and transport urgently
option_c: Give her water to drink for throat swelling
option_d: The swelling will go down on its own
rationale: Facial swelling difficulty breathing hives and hoarse voice after a sting indicate anaphylaxis with airway compromise. Epinephrine is the life-saving treatment. Assist with auto-injector or administer per protocol. High-flow oxygen and rapid transport are essential.

question: You assist a patient having an anaphylactic reaction with her EpiPen auto-injector. Where is the correct injection site and how do you administer it?
option_a: Inject into the deltoid muscle slowly
option_b: Inject into outer thigh through clothing if needed. Press firmly hold for 10 seconds. The EpiPen can be given through light clothing. Massage the site afterward. Monitor for continued symptoms requiring second dose
option_c: Inject into the buttock
rationale: EpiPen is administered into the outer thigh and can be given through light clothing. Press firmly until click is heard and hold for 10 seconds. Massage injection site. Continue monitoring as symptoms may return requiring second dose after 5-15 minutes.

question: A patient with a severe allergic reaction received epinephrine from her auto-injector. After 5 minutes her symptoms seem to be getting worse with increased stridor. What should you do?
option_a: Epinephrine did not work try something else
option_b: Repeat epinephrine if available and protocol allows. Anaphylaxis can require multiple doses. Continue high-flow oxygen and transport rapidly. Symptoms can recur as epinephrine wears off
option_c: Give antihistamines instead
option_d: Just continue transport without more medication
rationale: Anaphylaxis can be resistant to initial epinephrine requiring repeat doses. If patient has additional auto-injector and protocols allow give second dose. Epinephrine can be repeated every 5-15 minutes for persistent symptoms. Continue supportive care and transport.

question: A patient you have been treating for respiratory distress is now becoming less responsive. His respiratory rate has dropped from 30 to 12. He no longer responds to voice and his skin is becoming mottled and cyanotic. What is happening?
option_a: He is improving because his respiratory rate is lower
option_b: He is in respiratory failure. The decreased rate indicates exhaustion and impending respiratory arrest. His respiratory muscles are failing. Begin assisted ventilation with BVM immediately
option_c: Let him rest since he is tired
rationale: Decreasing respiratory rate in a patient who was working hard to breathe indicates exhaustion and respiratory failure. This is not improvement. The patient can no longer maintain ventilation. Begin immediate BVM ventilation with high-flow oxygen. This is a pre-arrest condition.

question: You are ventilating a patient with a BVM and oxygen. His SpO2 has improved from 70% to 92%. You are ventilating at 20 breaths per minute to try to get his oxygen higher. What should you adjust?
option_a: Continue at 20 breaths per minute until SpO2 is 100%
option_b: Reduce ventilation rate to 10-12 per minute. Over-ventilation causes gastric distension hypocapnia and can impair blood return to the heart. SpO2 of 92% is adequate. Ventilate at normal rate with adequate volume
option_c: Increase to 30 breaths per minute
option_d: Stop ventilating since SpO2 is better
rationale: Over-ventilation is harmful. Excessive rate causes gastric insufflation reduced CO2 which causes vasoconstriction and decreased venous return due to increased intrathoracic pressure. Ventilate at 10-12 per minute with adequate volume. SpO2 of 92%+ is acceptable.

question: You are ventilating an unresponsive patient with BVM but cannot achieve adequate chest rise despite repositioning. An OPA is in place. You have a supraglottic airway available. When should you consider using it?
option_a: Supraglottic airways are only for cardiac arrest
option_b: When BVM ventilation is inadequate despite proper technique and airway adjuncts a supraglottic airway should be considered. It can provide more effective ventilation than BVM when basic techniques fail
option_c: Never use on a patient with a pulse
option_d: Only paramedics can use supraglottic airways
rationale: Supraglottic airways like King LT or i-gel are indicated when BVM ventilation is inadequate. They are not limited to cardiac arrest. If you cannot effectively ventilate with BVM despite proper technique a supraglottic airway can be life-saving.

question: You respond to a patient with a tracheostomy who is in respiratory distress. The hole in his neck appears to have mucus in it. What should you do first?
option_a: Cover the stoma and ventilate through his mouth
option_b: Suction the tracheostomy stoma to clear secretions. For patients with tracheostomy or laryngectomy the stoma is their airway. Suctioning clears mucus plugging which is common. Provide oxygen through the stoma not mouth
option_c: Ignore the stoma and treat normally
option_d: Put an OPA in his mouth
rationale: Tracheostomy patients breathe through their stoma. Mucus plugging is common. Suction the stoma to clear secretions. Provide oxygen and ventilation through the stoma. If the patient has a total laryngectomy they cannot breathe through mouth or nose at all.

question: A patient with a tracheostomy is in severe respiratory distress. After suctioning he is still struggling. You need to ventilate him. How do you provide BVM ventilation?
option_a: Place mask over his face as usual
option_b: Ventilate through the stoma using a pediatric mask or special adapter over the tracheostomy opening. For laryngectomy patients seal the mouth and nose if needed to prevent air leak. The stoma is the only airway
option_c: Blow into the stoma with your mouth
rationale: BVM ventilation for tracheostomy patients is delivered through the stoma not the mouth. Use a pediatric mask or special adapter to create seal over the stoma. For total laryngectomy seal the mouth and nose to prevent air leak. For partial laryngectomy some air may pass through upper airway.

question: You arrive on scene and need to set up oxygen for a patient in respiratory distress. Your oxygen tank gauge shows 500 psi. The tank is an E-cylinder. Do you have enough oxygen and approximately how long will it last at 10 LPM?
option_a: The tank is almost empty. Find another tank
option_b: 500 psi provides approximately 30-35 minutes at 10 LPM for an E-cylinder. This should be sufficient for most responses but have backup available or request additional resources for extended calls
option_c: This will last several hours
option_d: Psi does not indicate how much oxygen remains
rationale: E-cylinder has approximately 0.28 liters per psi when full. At 500 psi you have approximately 140 liters. At 10 LPM this gives approximately 14 minutes of use. Consider backup oxygen for extended care or transport. Always check tank pressure before use.

question: You hear a hissing sound from your oxygen tank and regulator connection. What should you do?
option_a: This is normal. Oxygen always hisses
option_b: A hissing sound indicates a leak. Turn off the tank and check the connection. Ensure the washer is in place and not damaged. Retighten the connection. Leaking oxygen is both a resource waste and fire hazard
option_c: Tighten the connection with the oxygen flowing
option_d: Ignore it if oxygen is still coming out
rationale: Hissing indicates a gas leak at the regulator connection. Turn off tank check that washer is present and undamaged then reconnect and tighten. Leaking oxygen depletes your supply faster and is a fire hazard. Never use an oxygen system with leaks.

question: You are assessing breathing in a patient. You look listen and feel for 10 seconds. You see very slight chest rise hear faint breath sounds and feel minimal air at the mouth. Respiratory rate appears to be 6 per minute. How would you classify this breathing?
option_a: Breathing is present and adequate
option_b: Breathing is present but inadequate. Rate of 6 is too slow and minimal chest rise with faint sounds indicates poor tidal volume. This patient needs assisted ventilation with BVM
option_c: Normal breathing for a sleeping patient
option_d: No breathing since it is so faint
rationale: Breathing assessment considers presence rate and adequacy. This patient has breathing present but rate of 6 is dangerously slow and minimal volume is inadequate for gas exchange. This patient needs assisted ventilation to survive.

question: A patient is breathing 28 times per minute. Each breath is deep and full with obvious chest expansion. SpO2 is 98%. Is this breathing adequate?
option_a: Yes the rate is fast but breathing is adequate with good volume and oxygenation. Assess for cause of tachypnea such as fever pain anxiety or metabolic acidosis
option_b: No any rate above 20 requires assisted ventilation
option_c: This indicates respiratory failure
rationale: While the rate is elevated the breathing is effective with good chest rise and oxygenation. Fast rate with adequate depth and good SpO2 indicates the patient is compensating for some stimulus. Assess for the cause but do not interfere with effective breathing.

question: An unresponsive patient has occasional gasping breaths that are slow irregular and ineffective occurring only every 10-15 seconds. There is minimal chest movement. What is this breathing pattern and how should you manage it?
option_a: Normal breathing. Give oxygen by mask
option_b: Agonal respirations which are not effective breathing. This is a dying pattern often seen just before or after cardiac arrest. Treat as if not breathing by providing assisted ventilation with BVM. Check pulse if agonal breathing is present
option_c: Let patient continue breathing on their own
rationale: Agonal respirations are slow irregular gasping breaths that provide no effective ventilation. They are commonly seen in cardiac arrest and pre-arrest states. Do not be fooled into thinking the patient is breathing adequately. Provide BVM ventilation and check pulse.

question: You respond to a house fire and find a patient who was in a smoke-filled room. He is alert and talking. SpO2 reads 99%. He has soot around his nose and mouth. Should you be reassured by the normal SpO2?
option_a: Yes 99% means he is fine
option_b: No. Pulse oximetry cannot distinguish between oxygen and carbon monoxide on hemoglobin. Carbon monoxide poisoning can show normal or high SpO2 despite severe hypoxia. The soot indicates smoke exposure. Provide high-flow oxygen and transport
option_c: He does not need oxygen with that SpO2
rationale: Pulse oximetry measures what is bound to hemoglobin but cannot tell if it is oxygen or carbon monoxide. CO-hemoglobin reads the same as oxyhemoglobin. Smoke exposure victims may have CO poisoning with falsely normal SpO2. Give high-flow oxygen to all smoke inhalation patients.

question: An unconscious patient is breathing adequately on their own without intervention. You have no concerns for spinal injury. What position should you place them in?
option_a: Supine flat on their back
option_b: Recovery position lateral recumbent on their side. This position helps maintain airway patency and allows secretions or vomit to drain from the mouth rather than blocking the airway or being aspirated
option_c: Prone face down
rationale: The recovery position lateral recumbent is ideal for unconscious patients who are breathing adequately. It uses gravity to keep the tongue from obstructing and allows fluids to drain from the mouth. Monitor continuously as status can change.

question: A conscious patient in respiratory distress is most comfortable sitting bolt upright and leaning forward. She becomes more distressed when you try to lay her down for transport. How should you position her?
option_a: Insist she lie flat for transport
option_b: Allow her to remain sitting upright as this is the position of comfort for respiratory distress. Sitting reduces venous return to the heart and allows better diaphragm movement. Forcing her supine will worsen her breathing
option_c: She must lie flat for safety
rationale: Patients in respiratory distress typically breathe best sitting upright. This position allows maximum diaphragm excursion and reduces venous return which helps in pulmonary edema. Forcing a dyspneic patient supine can worsen their condition and cause severe distress.

question: A pregnant woman in her third trimester becomes dizzy and lightheaded when lying supine on the stretcher. Her blood pressure drops. What is causing this and how do you correct it?
option_a: She is having a stroke
option_b: Supine hypotension syndrome. The gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava when supine reducing blood return to heart. Position her on her left side or tilt the stretcher. If she must be supine manually displace the uterus to the left
option_c: She needs IV fluids immediately
rationale: In late pregnancy the heavy uterus can compress the IVC when supine reducing venous return and causing hypotension. Left lateral position or left lateral tilt displaces the uterus off the IVC. Manual displacement to the left also works if supine position is required.

question: You treated a patient with difficulty breathing. You gave oxygen and assisted with her inhaler. How should you document your airway interventions?
option_a: Just write gave oxygen and inhaler
option_b: Document initial respiratory status SpO2 work of breathing breath sounds. Document oxygen device liter flow and time started. Document inhaler assistance including medication dose puffs and technique. Document patient response and serial SpO2 values
option_c: Documentation is not important for breathing calls
option_d: Only document if something went wrong
rationale: Thorough documentation includes initial assessment findings all interventions with times doses and methods and patient response. For respiratory care document oxygen device flow rate and SpO2 before and after. For inhaler assistance document medication and patient technique and response.
